description: Whether the option increases safe upstream primary, urgent and ambulance capacity.
Whether the option reduces avoidable ED, ambulance conveyance and hospital admission flow.
Whether access expands without worsening inequity, trust, Maori/Pacific provider legitimacy or co-payment barriers.
Whether the option protects local in-person capacity rather than substituting telehealth only.
Whether the model is affordable, controllable and transparent to central fiscal decision-makers.
Whether it avoids avoidable churn, opportunistic claiming and provider-induced demand.
Whether it lowers transaction costs and barriers to market entry or expansion.
Whether scope, prescribing, referral, audit, clinical governance and accountability controls are strong.
Whether the option can survive institutional contestation and be framed coherently.
Whether outcomes, contacts and downstream flows can be measured and reported at top-tier level.
